title: Co-Branded Airline/Hotel Card BIN Exploitation via Compromised Test Accounts
narrative: Fraudsters exploit co-branded credit card BINs (airline/hotel rewards cards) by targeting test or dormant accounts within specific IIN ranges. Attack involves 8-15 transactions over 2-4 hours at travel MCCs (4511, 4722, 3501-3999) with amounts clustering near $287-$412 to avoid single-transaction limits. Attackers leverage legitimate P_emaildomains matching card issuer partnerships (e.g., united.com, marriott.com) but pair with mismatched R_emaildomains (temp mail services). Transactions show card1_txn_count_1day jumping from 0 to 12+, all originating from 2-3 addr1 values despite dist1 showing 850+ miles between claimed billing and shipping. CVV match rate near 100% suggests full card data compromise. Timing concentrated in is_night=1 windows (txn_hour 1-5) when fraud monitoring may be reduced. ProductCD always 'W' (website) despite merchant being travel aggregator.
indicators: card1 first 6 digits match co-branded issuer BIN, card1_txn_count_1day = 0 before attack then 8-15 within 4 hours
TransactionAmt clusters $287-$412 across 80%+ of transactions, all at MCC 4511/4722/3501-3999
P_emaildomain matches card partnership brand but R_emaildomain is guerrillamail.com, tempmail.net, or similar
dist1 > 850 miles while addr1 repeats across 70%+ of burst transactions
txn_hour concentrated 1-5 AM (is_night=1) with card1_amt_zscore jumping from -0.2 to 4.8+ within session
CVV2 match rate 95-100% but AVS shows partial/no match on 60%+ of transactions
summary: A burst of 8-15 travel purchases ($287-$412 each) hits a co-branded airline or hotel card in the middle of the night, all from the same shipping address despite the billing address being 850+ miles away. The purchaser email matches the card brand's partner domain, but the recipient email is a temporary mail service.
reasoning: Fraudsters have stolen full card details (including CVV) from a dormant rewards account and are racing to buy travel services before the cardholder notices, using the brand-matching email to appear legitimate while shipping to a controlled address and hiding behind reduced overnight monitoring.
indicator_explanations: Co-branded card appears dormant then erupts with rapid transactions in single session
Purchase amounts cluster in narrow band hitting travel merchant categories repeatedly
Billing email matches card brand but reply address uses disposable provider
Transactions span huge geographic range while shipping to identical repeated address
Attack unfolds in dead-of-night hours with amounts spiking far above card baseline
CVV verification succeeds perfectly but billing addresses fail verification consistently
